payload.text: I would like to be able to speak in natural language concerning things that I need to do and have these things added to a list of other to do's that I have mentioned previously. I would like to have the AI to listen for a description of the task, when it might be due, and what times to schedule an alarm or multiple alarms to make sure I do not forget. If possible also listen for locations, so that the alert would go off each time I return home from work for example. Lastly I would like for the actual alarm to just be an AI voice reading out the description of the task or the to-do item.

**Initiating Design Concepts**

I'm currently focused on the initial design of a React application for a voice-activated to-do list, using the Gemini Live API for native audio input. I've begun to conceptualize how the user interface will respond to voice commands, ensuring a smooth and intuitive experience for adding tasks through natural language processing.


**Developing Core Functionality**

I'm now diving into the core AI functionality. My focus is on extracting key data points from user voice input, specifically the task description, due dates, alarm schedules, and location triggers. I'm also considering the best approach for the alarm/notification system. While native browser alarms have limitations, I'll implement an in-app check using `setInterval` to trigger alarms, and I'll use either the Gemini TTS API or browser's `speechSynthesis` for the AI voice.


**Evaluating Alarm Options**

I'm now zeroing in on the alarm mechanism. Initially, I thought I could reuse the Live API for voice readouts, but considering session state management, pre-generating the alarm audio with `gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts` seems a safer, more "AI" approach. Alternatively, if I don't want to use Gemini TTS, the standard browser TTS can always be a fallback to ensure reliable alarm delivery.


**Structuring the Application**

I've been mapping out the application architecture. The main container will be `App.tsx`, managing an array of tasks and the Gemini Live session. Components like `LiveAudioVisualizer`, `TaskList`, `AlarmManager`, and `ControlPanel` will handle specific functionalities. I'm focusing on a clean separation of concerns, ensuring each component has a well-defined role in the application. I'm starting to think `AlarmManager` should be implemented as a background hook.


**Integrating Voice Processing**

I'm now integrating the voice-to-text pipeline. Raw PCM data from `MediaRecorder` will be sent to the Gemini Live API session. The AI output will then undergo audio decoding and playback through an `AudioContext`. I'm also incorporating the use of tool definitions, especially `addTask`, which includes fields for due dates, alarm times, and location triggers.  I'm also working on location logic, and how to best get the user's geofence. I'm focusing on user experience, and will likely simplify the location feature for this demonstration by just capturing the *intent* of the trigger.


**Prioritizing Location Logic**

I've been wrestling with how best to handle location-based reminders. Initially, I considered complex geofencing with a backend, but for this demo, I'll streamline it. The AI will extract a `locationDescription` from the voice input, like "home" or "work". The app will then grab the user's current coordinates using `navigator.geolocation` and display them, demonstrating the ability to capture location intent without full geofencing implementation. My focus will be on time-based alarms and natural conversation. I'm also planning to pre-generate alarm audio using `gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts` for a reliable "It's time to..." notification.


**Organizing App Structure**

I've decided to refine the overall app structure further. Now, I'm focusing on refining the live API connection. The goal is a more conversational interface, enabling follow-up commands. This means maintaining the session state and ensuring the correct tool responses are relayed back to the model using `session.sendToolResponse`. I'm going to start by setting up the HTML/index structure, then define the necessary types for `Task` and `LiveSessionState`. After that, I will implement audio utilities for playback.


**Defining Live API Interactions**

I'm now fully immersed in the core live API interactions. I'm focusing on handling `onmessage` and `onopen` events for audio playback and mic stream management. I'm implementing the `addTask` and `deleteTask` tools, integrating them into the Live API context. I'm prioritizing concise responses and smooth user interactions. I've defined my state with `tasks` and plan to use an `useEffect` to implement an alarm check. Also, I'll store location data as a description and display it, simplifying the location trigger.


**Implementing Alarm Simulation**

I'm now integrating an alarm simulation feature.  I'll add a "Simulate Arriving Home" button that triggers a location-based alert to demonstrate the feature, responding to commands like "Remind me when I get home". I'm also planning to utilize `gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts` for the alarm readout, aligning with the prompt's request for an AI voice. I'll follow the guidelines for Live API and audio decoding.


**Refining Task Management**

I'm now revising my tool definitions for the Gemini Live API. I'm moving toward distinct `addTask` and `removeTask` functions to improve model accuracy. These will include parameters for task details, due dates, alarm times, and location triggers. I'm focusing on ensuring clear communication with the AI.


**Developing Core Audio Handling**

I'm currently working on `utils/audioUtils.ts`, needing base64 encoding/decoding and PCM conversion for seamless audio data handling within the application. I'm also drafting the foundational `App.tsx` and `MicrophoneInput` components, including state management for tasks, audio context initialization, and the real-time input stream for Live API interactions. I'm considering using an effect hook to handle alarms and `setInterval` to check for expired tasks.

payload.text: Enhance the alarm functionality to support recurring alarms. When a user specifies a recurring alarm (e.g., 'every day at 9 AM'), schedule it accordingly and ensure the AI can manage these recurring events.

**Developing Recurring Alarms**

I'm currently focused on the `Task` interface, specifically the `alarmTime` field. **Defining Recurrence Patterns**

I'm now thinking about how to represent the concept of recurrence. I've decided to include a `recurrence` field in the `Task` interface as a string, initially supporting patterns like 'daily', 'weekly', 'weekdays', and 'monthly'. This will allow me to capture the recurring nature of alarms. The next step is to integrate this new field into the `addTask` tool definition and the `handleToolCall` function, ensuring that the AI can understand and process the recurrence parameter when creating tasks. I also need to update the alarm-checking logic to handle these recurring events correctly, especially to calculate the `next` occurrence time.


**Refining Alarm Recurrence**

payload.text: Implement functionality to trigger alarms based on the user's current location, using the Geolocation API.

**Integrating Location Tracking**

**Refining Location Handling**

I'm presently refining the `locationTrigger` within `types.ts`. I'm leaning towards directly using the Geolocation API for proximity-based alarms, simplifying the "home" and "work" concept for now.  This means transitioning from a string match to comparing real-time user location with saved target coordinates.  My goal is to make the location triggers more responsive and accurate, while avoiding overcomplication.


**Mapping Location Logic**

I'm currently considering how to bridge location data to alarm triggers. I'm focusing on simplicity, so maybe a UI element to set "Home" location via a button click is the way. Adding a `coordinateTrigger` might be the simplest solution.


**Implementing Location Triggers**

I'm now integrating location data into alarm triggers. My current plan is to keep the LLM interaction simple, using string-based location triggers like "Home" or "Work." When the user is within a small radius of a saved location, tasks with that label will be triggered.


**Developing Location-Based Triggers**

I'm now implementing the distance calculation using the Haversine formula and considering state management for location-based triggers. I need to avoid constantly re-triggering alarms when the user is within the threshold. I'm focusing on "entry" triggering. I'll use a boolean state object, `isAtLocation`, and persist it.  I'm also exploring using a `processedLocationAlarms` reference to manage alarm triggers.


**Developing Location Utilities**

I'm currently creating `utils/location.ts` for distance calculations using the Haversine formula, so location logic remains separate from `App.tsx`. The existing `addTaskTool` supports `locationTrigger`, but needs adjustments for real coordinates, and `simulateLocationArrival` will be replaced or refactored. I am adding a new util file, or keep it inside `App.tsx`.


**Enhancing Geolocation Integration**

I'm now making more substantial code changes, starting with `utils/geo.ts` for distance calculations using the Haversine formula, and then integrating this logic into `App.tsx`. I've added state for `savedLocations` and persistent storage for them, and implemented `watchPosition` logic to monitor user location. I'm checking distances against saved locations (Home/Work) and triggering location arrivals when a match is found. I've also added UI elements to set "Home" and "Work" coordinates.


**Defining Core Coordinates**

I'm now integrating the Haversine formula from `utils/geo.ts` into my app, along with a `SavedLocations` type in `types.ts` to manage home and work coordinates. I'm importing `calculateDistance` in `App.tsx` and creating state for `savedLocations`. I'm using an effect to load and save these coordinates to local storage and I'm setting up `navigator.geolocation.watchPosition` to track the user's location. Inside this function, I'm calculating the distance to saved locations.

I'm introducing states like `atHome` and `atWork` to track the user's current location status. This will help trigger tasks only when transitioning into a new state. I will use the code from before as a starting point, and integrate it into `watchPosition` to ensure location states and alarms trigger correctly.


**Improving Trigger Robustness**

I'm presently tackling a more robust location-based trigger system, focusing on preventing excessive alarm repetition. I'm leveraging a ref, `isAtLocationRef`, to track the user's presence at "Home" or "Work", and integrating "cooldown" logic. If the user moves beyond a hysteresis threshold, I'll reset the state. I'm also ensuring all changes are persistent and will review the App.tsx modifications to test the integration of all updates.


**Refactoring Simulation Logic**

I'm now refactoring the simulation logic, recognizing the need to replace or augment it with real geolocation. I'm focusing on the Geolocation API as the primary trigger, not a secondary source. I'll phase out the simulation as the user's location is now the central input for alarm activation. My next step will be to remove the current implementation.

Based on your request, I will implement real-time location tracking using the Geolocation API. This will allow the application to trigger "Home" or "Work" tasks when you physically arrive at those locations.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`utils/geo.ts`**: A new utility file to calculate the distance between two geographical coordinates (Haversine formula).
2.  **`types.ts`**: Added definitions for `Coordinates` and `SavedLocations` to manage user-defined locations.

    *   Added state to store "Home" and "Work" coordinates, persisted in local storage.
    *   Implemented `navigator.geolocation.watchPosition` to monitor your real-time location.

    *   Added UI buttons under a new "Location Settings" section to allow you to set your current physical location as "Home" or "Work".

**Elaborating Verification Paths**

I've detailed two methods for bypassing the "unverified app" screen, one temporary for testing and another for development use. I've broken down "Advanced" options for immediate testing and outlined steps for the Google Cloud Console's OAuth consent screen setup. I'm prioritizing clarity in explaining each of the steps involved in each method.


**Describing the App Review Process**

I'm now detailing the rigorous requirements for full OAuth consent screen verification. This encompasses essential elements like a privacy policy, a demonstration video, and a time-consuming review. I will also explain that this is probably not the path the user wants to take, unless they are ready for the time investment. My objective is to offer a comprehensive, user-friendly guide.

You are seeing that warning because your Google Cloud Project is currently in "Testing" mode and hasn't been reviewed by Google's compliance team. This is completely normal for personal projects and development.

You have two ways to handle this:

### Option 1: Bypass the Warning (Fastest)
You don't actually need to verify the app to use it yourself.
1. When the "Google hasn't verified this app" screen appears, click the **Advanced** link (usually in the bottom-left corner).
2. Click **Go to [Your App Name] (unsafe)** at the very bottom.
3. Grant the permissions, and it will work.

### Option 2: Add Yourself as a Test User (Recommended)
To prevent issues with logging in, ensure your email is explicitly allowed.
1. Go to the [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com/).
2. Navigate to **APIs & Services** > **OAuth consent screen**.
3. Under the **Test users** section, click **+ ADD USERS**.
4. Enter your specific Google email address and click **Save**.

**Note on Full Verification:**
Actually "verifying" the app is a long process intended for public production apps. It requires submitting a privacy policy, a YouTube video demonstration, and waiting weeks for Google's trust and safety team to review your code. For this prototype, **Option 1** is the standard path.

The "Error 400: invalid_request" with that specific security message usually means one of two things:

1.  **Origin Mismatch:** The URL in your browser address bar (e.g., `http://localhost:3000` or `http://127.0.0.1:5173`) does not **exactly** match the **Authorized JavaScript origins** in your Google Cloud Console. Note that `localhost` and `127.0.0.1` are treated as different origins.
2.  **Insecure Origin:** You are trying to log in from a custom domain or IP address using `http://` instead of `https://`. Google blocks OAuth on insecure origins (except for localhost).

3.  Go to Google Cloud Console > APIs & Services > Credentials.
4.  Edit your OAuth 2.0 Client ID.
5.  Paste that URL into **"Authorized JavaScript origins"** and click Save.
6.  **Wait 5 minutes** (updates can take a few moments to propagate).
